topic: Gravity at the Rim: 90% Drop — self-contradiction section
our_argument_summary: Section 4.5.3 argues that the dome's aetheric circulation in a toroidal loop should produce lower gravity at the Antarctic rim due to reduced aetheric pressure (invoking the Ekman spiral analogy). Measurements show gravity at the South Pole is slightly higher than at the equator, opposite to the dome prediction. The author resolves this by using the globe formula g = GM/r² instead of deriving gravity from his aetheric model.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No gravity formula appears in the dome model's published parameters. The dome model page lists H(r) = 8537×exp(−r/8619) and a mag-gravity coupling constant (1.67 nT/μGal) but no explicit g(r) formula. The DOCX section 4.5.2 references g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/8619)) as the dome's gravity curve fit, but this formula's provenance from the dome site needs verification.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — this is a prose section review
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome model never explicitly states that gravity arises from aetheric pressure columns. This is our inference from the model's architecture, which is reasonable but should be flagged as interpretation.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None for code — but the section claims the dome uses 'the globe formula g = GM / r²' which appears to be wrong; the DOCX section says the author uses a curve fit g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/8619)). These are different formulas.

holes_found.description: Section numbering mismatch between HTML and DOCX. HTML section 4.5.3 is 'Gravity at the Rim: 90% Drop' but DOCX section 4.5.3 is 'Solar Angular Diameter: 29× Too Much Variation'. The DOCX puts gravity content at 4.5.2 as 'Gravity Variation: 152× Too Large'. This means readers of the two documents see different content at the same section number, and cross-references between documents are broken.
holes_found.recommendation: Align section numbering between HTML (generate-html.js) and DOCX (build-doc-v4.js). Ideally, extract section ordering into a shared data structure so both outputs are guaranteed to match.

holes_found.description: HTML section 4.5.3 is dramatically weaker than its DOCX counterpart (4.5.2). The DOCX computes H(r) at pole and equator, derives the 152× ratio, and shows the curve-fit formula. The HTML section makes only a qualitative argument about fluid pressure and Ekman spirals, never computing H(rim)/H(pole) = 837/8537 = 0.098 to show the 90% drop. Despite the title promising '90% Drop', the section never calculates it. A dome defender could dismiss the HTML version as hand-waving while being unable to dismiss the DOCX's quantitative argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Add the quantitative H(r) calculation to the HTML section: H(pole) = 8,537 km, H(rim) = 837 km, ratio = 0.098, i.e. 90% drop. Also add the curve-fit formula observation. The title says '90% Drop' — the text should show the math that produces it.

holes_found.description: The section claims the dome author 'resolves this by not calculating gravity from his aetheric circulation model and instead using the globe formula g = GM / r².' But this appears to be wrong. The DOCX section says the author uses g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/8619)), which is NOT the globe formula g = GM/r². It's a curve fit using the observed equatorial gravity constant as a starting value and the dome's decay parameter λ=8619. A dome defender could correctly point out that we misidentified which formula the author uses, undermining our credibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'the globe formula g = GM / r²' with an accurate description of what the dome model actually uses for gravity. If the curve-fit formula can be confirmed from the dome site, cite it. If not, state that the author does not derive gravity from his aetheric model but instead fits to observed values.

holes_found.description: The Ekman spiral analogy is a stretch. The Ekman spiral is a specific oceanographic phenomenon involving wind-driven currents interacting with the Coriolis force in a rotating fluid. It is not a general principle about circulating fluids losing pressure at the periphery. A dome defender could call this a misapplication of a geophysics concept. The general principle we're invoking — that pressure drops in a radially-spreading flow — is correct but doesn't need the Ekman spiral label, which adds a false air of specificity.
holes_found.recommendation: Either remove the Ekman spiral reference and state the general fluid dynamics principle directly (pressure decreases with distance from the driving source in any circulating flow), or qualify it as an analogy rather than a direct application. The argument is stronger without the questionable reference.

holes_found.description: Unsourced attribution of dome's gravity mechanism. The section claims the dome model uses 'aetheric circulation in a toroidal loop, with circulating aether providing local gravity' and that 'the author states the south polar region is where the aetheric flow descends and returns.' I could not find these specific claims in the raw-text files. The dome model describes an 'aetheric vortex' in the context of magnetic field behavior (SAA, NMP) but doesn't explicitly connect aetheric circulation to gravity. We also attribute the dome saying Antarctica is where aetheric flow 'descends and returns' but this appears to be our extrapolation, not a direct quote.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) cite the specific dome page where the toroidal gravity mechanism is described, or (b) reframe as 'The dome's architecture implies...' rather than 'The author states...' to avoid the appearance of strawmanning. The dome model page describes 'aetheric medium' filling the cavity but doesn't explicitly claim it produces gravity via pressure columns.

holes_found.description: Significant redundancy with HTML section 4.5.8 ('Gravity Gradient with Latitude'). Section 4.5.8 discusses the exponential gravity profile g(r) = g₀ × exp(−r/8619), predicting a 50% drop by r = 6,000 km and a 90% drop at the rim. Both 4.5.3 and 4.5.8 argue that the dome geometry predicts far too much gravity variation. Having two sections making essentially the same argument (one qualitatively, one quantitatively) weakens the overall impression — it looks like we're padding the section count rather than making distinct arguments.
holes_found.recommendation: Merge sections 4.5.3 and 4.5.8 into a single gravity self-contradiction section. Lead with the quantitative H(r) calculation (currently in 4.5.8), then note the additional problem that the aetheric circulation mechanism would produce lower pressure at the periphery (currently in 4.5.3). One strong section > two weak ones.

holes_found.description: The section states 'Measurements show gravity at the South Pole (~9.83 m/s²) is actually slightly higher than at the equator (~9.78 m/s²), opposite to the dome prediction.' This framing is slightly misleading. The dome predicts gravity decreases monotonically from the North Pole (r=0) to the rim (r=20,015). The fact that South Pole gravity is higher than equatorial gravity isn't 'opposite' to the dome prediction — the rim is farther from center than the equator. The real contradiction is that South Pole gravity equals North Pole gravity (both ~9.83), when the dome predicts a factor-of-10 difference between them.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe to emphasize the pole-to-pole comparison: 'Gravity at the North Pole (~9.83 m/s²) and South Pole (~9.83 m/s²) are nearly identical, yet the dome's H(r) predicts H=8,537 km at the North Pole and H=837 km at the rim — a 10× difference that should produce a dramatic gravity gradient which does not exist.'

holes_found.description: No cross-reference to WIN-067 (Antarctic gravity hole), which makes the same H(r)-based argument in much greater quantitative detail. The section and the WIN cover very similar ground. Readers who see the section may not know to expand WIN-067 for the full calculation, and vice versa.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a cross-reference: 'See WIN-067 for the full quantitative derivation.'

stronger_arguments.description: The DOCX section 4.5.2 notes the dome author uses g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/8619)), where 9.7803 is the observed equatorial gravity. This is devastating: the 'prediction' starts from the known answer and curve-fits using the dome's decay parameter. The HTML section should highlight this circularity — the dome doesn't predict gravity, it assumes it.

The strongest version of this argument is the direct H(r) ratio: H(pole)/H(rim) = 8537/837 ≈ 10.2. Any physical mechanism where gravity scales with the aetheric column height (which is the only interpretation consistent with 'aetheric pressure') predicts a factor of ~10 in g from pole to rim. Observed: factor of 1.005. This is a clean, model-independent contradiction that doesn't rely on any assumptions about fluid dynamics or Ekman spirals.

Consider the flip side: a dome defender could argue that gravity doesn't arise from aetheric pressure at all, and that the dome model doesn't claim this. If so, the dome model has no gravity mechanism and cannot make any gravity predictions — which undermines WIN-011, WIN-012, WIN-013, WIN-014, and WIN-067. Either the aether produces gravity (and H(r) contradicts observations) or it doesn't (and the gravity WINs have no dome-derived basis). This dilemma could be made explicit.

kernel_of_truth.description: The concept of a position-dependent gravitational field arising from a medium (rather than from mass distribution) has a faint echo in general relativity, where spacetime curvature (the 'medium') determines gravitational effects. The dome's intuition that the local 'medium density' should affect gravitational acceleration is not crazy in principle — it's just that the dome's specific geometry (H(r) varying by 10×) makes predictions wildly incompatible with observation.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Even granting the concept, the dome's own H(r) function is quantitatively wrong by a factor of ~170× on gravity variation. And the dome author doesn't actually derive gravity from H(r) — he curve-fits to observations, defeating the purpose of having a physical model.

deeper_analysis:
This section is the weakest of the self-contradiction subsections. The Schumann (4.5.1), tidal (4.5.2), and solar diameter (4.5.4) sections all make crisp, quantitative arguments grounded in the dome's own stated parameters. Section 4.5.3, by contrast, makes a qualitative fluid-dynamics argument with a questionable analogy (Ekman spiral) and an incorrect formula attribution (g = GM/r²). The irony is that the gravity self-contradiction is one of our STRONGEST arguments when presented quantitatively (as in the DOCX and WIN-067), yet the HTML section presenting it is the weakest version. This is a significant quality gap.

Additionally, the section numbering mismatch between HTML and DOCX means anyone trying to cross-check our arguments between formats will be confused. The HTML has gravity at 4.5.3 and solar diameter at 4.5.4; the DOCX has gravity at 4.5.2 and solar diameter at 4.5.3. This is exactly the kind of inconsistency that makes a review look sloppy.

From a dome-defender perspective, the most effective attack on this section would be: 'They claim I use g = GM/r² — I don't. They invoke the Ekman spiral — which applies to wind-driven ocean currents, not aetheric circulation. They attribute claims to me that I never made. This is a strawman.' And they'd be partially right. The underlying argument (H(r) implies too much gravity variation) is sound, but the HTML presentation is vulnerable.

citations_failed: The claim that the dome uses 'the globe formula g = GM / r²' could not be verified. The dome model page does not list this formula for gravity. The DOCX section references g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/8619)) instead.
citations_unchecked: South Pole gravity ~9.83 m/s² and equator ~9.78 m/s² — these are standard textbook values and likely correct, but no specific source is cited in the section.
The claim that 'the author states the south polar region is where the aetheric flow descends and returns' — could not find this specific statement in extracted raw-text files.
